serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw bone-in lamb shank weighs approximately 350 g, yielding roughly 200–220 g of edible lean meat after cooking and bone removal.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, New Zealand, imported, shank, separable lean and fat, raw
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore database values (energy 188 kcal, protein 21.7 g, fat 11.2 g, carbohydrate 0 g, sodium 95 mg, potassium 306 mg, calcium 13 mg, iron 1.65 mg, cholesterol 69 mg) are retained as primary source per input provenance (HPB, confidence 0.95). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw lamb shank (separable lean and fat) is used as corroborating secondary source and to supply saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and additional micronutrient values. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw lamb shank contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this is a pure protein and fat food with no digestible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI testing is performed on pure protein/fat foods. The numeric value 0 is used here as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; the gi_category of not_applicable clarifies the clinical interpretation.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (95 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (306 mg K : 95 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Raw lamb shank is low in sodium at 95 mg/100 g and provides 306 mg potassium/100 g, yielding a potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 3.2:1, which supports blood pressure management; however, saturated fat (4.9 g/100 g) should be minimised by trimming visible fat before cooking, and high-sodium braising liquids or commercial stocks must be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (21.7 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw lamb shank contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, producing no postprandial glycaemic response; its high protein content (21.7 g/100 g) supports satiety and lean mass preservation, making it suitable for type 2 diabetes meal plans when prepared without sugar-containing glazes or sweet braising sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.9 g/100 g and cholesterol 69 mg/100 g; trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 4.9 g/100 g and cholesterol at 69 mg/100 g are moderate concerns for cardiovascular disease; trimming all visible external fat before cooking, limiting portion size to 100–150 g cooked lean meat, and avoiding high-saturated-fat cooking methods such as deep frying are recommended to reduce LDL-raising lipid exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.9 g/100 g; arachidonic acid (omega-6) content; trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw lamb shank is a red meat with elevated saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with active RA should trim all visible fat, limit consumption frequency, and consider substituting with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish or legumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb shank, raw, is the uncooked lower leg portion of a young sheep (lamb), sold bone-in with lean muscle meat and both internal and external fat attached. The cut displays a deep red colour with a dense, firm texture characteristic of well-exercised leg muscles. Raw lamb shank is a collagen-rich cut that becomes tender through slow, moist-heat cooking methods such as braising, slow-roasting, or pressure cooking, which convert the abundant connective tissue into gelatin. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 188 kcal, 21.7 g protein, 11.2 g total fat (of which approximately 4.9 g is saturated), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 95 mg sodium, 306 mg potassium, 13 mg calcium, 1.65 mg iron, and 69 mg cholesterol. The zero carbohydrate content makes it suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic meal plans. The moderate saturated fat content warrants fat trimming for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Lamb shank is widely used in Middle Eastern, South Asian, Mediterranean, and Mamak (Malaysian-Indian Muslim) cuisines, commonly braised with aromatic spices such as cumin, coriander, cinnamon, and turmeric. It is halal-certified when sourced from halal-slaughtered animals. The cut is a good source of zinc (3.9 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg/100 g), niacin (5.7 mg/100 g), and phosphorus (175 mg/100 g), supporting immune function, red blood cell formation, and energy metabolism.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore database with confidence 0.95 and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw lamb shank (separable lean and fat). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by named approved sources. No estimation from unrelated food categories was required.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb shank is a pure animal muscle meat with no carbohydrate-containing ingredients. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as dietary fibre is absent in animal muscle tissue. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB database for this cut; USDA SR Legacy reports negligible retinol activity for lamb shank lean-and-fat raw. Folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported in HPB database; limited published data for this specific cut. Vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB database for raw lamb shank. Vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB database for raw lamb shank. Trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid precursors), not industrially produced; this is distinct from industrial trans fat but is noted as a pro-inflammatory factor for rheumatoid arthritis management. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (lamb shank, separable lean and fat, raw) as HPB database does not report these fields for this entry. Primary cuisine classification is Mamak per input record; food is also widely used in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and Mediterranean cuisines. Kosher status is not confirmed as lamb shank requires specific kosher slaughter and salting procedures; is_kosher set to false pending certification confirmation. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value to satisfy the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to clarify that GI testing is not relevant for this zero-carbohydrate food. This is consistent with the clinical interpretation that raw lamb shank produces no glycaemic response.
